excerpt_en: June 2, 2025: Since yesterday's entry was all about business, today I'll talk about the London visit on June 2nd. After finishing a meeting about setting up a Dubai corporation in the morning, I headed to V&A Eas
body_en:
June 2, 2025:

Since yesterday's entry was all about business, today I'll talk about the London visit on June 2nd.

After finishing a meeting about setting up a Dubai corporation in the morning, I headed to V&A East Storehouse (Victoria & Albert Museum East Storehouse).

This facility had just opened on May 31st. I visited on only the third day after its opening!

This new facility opens up approximately 250,000 previously unseen collection items to the public, allowing visitors to see storage areas and restoration work up close—an experience quite different from traditional museums.

The idea of turning an actual warehouse into a museum really resonated with FOMUS and our masu. The concept of a mere storage space taking on meaning as a museum is a really fascinating idea.

Since the famous works are displayed at other museums, there wasn't anything I particularly recognized, but I thought this unique approach was wonderful. FOMUS also has experience renovating an empty shipping container into an art gallery, so I felt a real connection there.

Liberty: Learning from a Design Mecca

Next, I visited London's long-established luxury department store, **Liberty.

Liberty is renowned worldwide as a design mecca that fuses tradition with artistry.

GPT told me it was "a place packed with hints for world-building and high-end product development for a brand like FOMUS that's interested in craft, fabric, art, jewelry, and lifestyle goods," so I thought I absolutely had to go and made my way there.

As expected of a luxury department store, every item exuded a sense of luxury. In particular, the Liberty paper bags you get when you buy something are incredibly stylish on their own and left a real impression! I didn't buy anything since I didn't want extra luggage, but it really drove home the importance of packaging design for gifts and presents.

Currently, FOMUS masu don't come with a dedicated box, but I strongly felt that we absolutely need to create one to convey a sense of luxury. I found several packaging designs that could serve as references, so I'd like to develop packaging that perfectly fits the FOMUS brand.

Also, there was a design exhibition on the top floor today, where they talked about Liberty's "DNA."

"I AM. WE ARE. LIBERTY."

"I am Liberty. We are Liberty."

Liberty constantly reinvents itself.

Its DNA—namely Design, Nature, Art**—remains faithful to these pillars while continuing to evolve in a cyclical journey that circles back to its origins and archetypes.

Through this process, Liberty weaves past, present, and future together like a single living thread, remaining perpetually relevant and resonant with the times.

Since its founding in 1875, Liberty has served as a laboratory for creativity, generating innovation, invention, and unique expression. This exists within a process of ceaselessly iterating on the evolution of form and thought.

Liberty's DNA of "Design, Nature, Art" turned out to be keywords that deeply resonated with FOMUS as well.

In fact, I felt it would be worth "redefining as our own DNA" and adopting it as a way to organize FOMUS's philosophy.

So I came up with my own version of FOMUS's three DNA pillars.

  • Design

─ The design of "function and beauty" that lives in the shape of the masu, spaces, products, and adornment

  • Nature

─ The life of wood, forests, fragrance, earth, recycled materials... the cycle of life and stories dwelling in everything

  • Art

─ Self-expression, photography, accessories, stories, and creativity as a "vessel that connects with the world"

This might be a great way to communicate our brand to an international audience.

These kinds of discoveries are something you can only gain by actually going there in person. When you keep your antenna up, sometimes you stumble upon these moments that just hit you.

This connects to FOMUS's brand story, concept, and statement, and it turned out to be an incredibly meaningful experience!

title_en: Last Day in London: Laying the Groundwork for the Future
excerpt_en: June 3, 2025: Day 4 of my London stay, essentially the last full day. In the morning, I revisited Japan House, Japan's flagship facility in London that I had also visited on day one. They exhibit and sell Japanese traditional crafts and outstanding content, and as I
body_en:
June 3, 2025:

Day 4 of my London stay,

essentially the last full day.

In the morning, I revisited Japan House, Japan's flagship facility in London that I had also visited on day one.

They exhibit and sell Japanese traditional crafts and outstanding content, and as I mentioned in a previous entry, they also carry masu.

However, it wouldn't mean much for FOMUS to simply propose the same kind of masu. What I want to place here in London is the masu jewelry brand "FOMUS PARURE."

FOMUS PARURE can be used as accessories, interior decor, and art. I genuinely believed that even just displaying it at Japan House could serve as content that further conveys the beauty of Japan.

Of course, I didn't have any connections, so I went ahead and asked a staff member directly. "How would I go about getting something displayed here?"

One of my strengths is the ability to just walk in and take action. I rarely make appointments, and since I only learned about this place after arriving in London, it was too short notice to set one up. So I figured asking directly was the fastest way.

They told me that since they have a lot of different content right now, I should first reach out by email.

In other words, if I send an email and they're even slightly interested, they'll hear me out.

That's totally fine by me.

Over the next few days, I plan to customize FOMUS PARURE materials specifically for Japan House and send them an email.

If I see a place with my own eyes and think "I want my stuff here!", I follow the proper steps. And if I feel there's no time for that, I dive right in (like with the Cramaítre I visited the other day).

What I can do right now is visit places in person as MaSU, the representative of FOMUS, gather information, and leverage AI to the fullest to create materials tailored to each specific project.

And if a presentation is needed, I prepare thoroughly before going in. That's pretty much my approach.

From this London visit,

this was truly a major takeaway.

National Gallery:

Efficient Art Appreciation Using AI

After Japan House, I walked about an hour to the National Gallery.

By the way, I make it a point to walk at least 10,000 steps a day for my health. While walking, I listen to Voicy to study, or check music I've been producing, so I'm working even while on the move.

The National Gallery is Britain's flagship national art museum located at Trafalgar Square in London. It boasts one of the world's finest collections of Western European paintings, with over 2,300 works from the late 13th century to the early 20th century on display.

Since I only wanted to focus on the famous works, I had AI (P-chan) list out the notable pieces, and I centered my visit around those.

I think this is a new way of enjoying museums by leveraging AI. Personally, I had a great time!

London Japanese Meetup: The Importance of Cross-Cultural Exchange

In the evening, I attended a Japanese meetup in London.

Apparently this event is held every first Tuesday of the month. Since my stay just happened to extend through this first Tuesday, I went to check out the atmosphere and see if there were any interesting people.

I learned about this meetup thanks to P-chan as well.

When I got there, it was roughly a fifty-fifty split between Japanese people and people from other countries. The rule of this meetup is "basically speak in Japanese," and many of the non-Japanese attendees spoke Japanese remarkably well.

I chatted with various people—some who had used Japanese scholarships to study abroad for six months after COVID, others who had done homestays or internships. Everyone had this amazing combination of being "talented and loving Japan."

People asked me a lot of questions too, so I shared what I could. For some reason, the story about organizing my own events in Ireland and gathering people to practice English got a big laugh.

There were also people planning to stay for two months to study English, someone on a one-year overseas assignment from the Ministry of Justice, artists, and others with all sorts of backgrounds. Interacting with such a diverse group of people was truly a valuable experience.

I also got to meet the event organizer. Amazingly, they've been running this event for over 25 years. That's incredible. When I shared a bit about what I do, they seemed genuinely intrigued.

These kinds of networking events really do attract local people, and diving in allows you to see so much.

It rarely leads directly to business right away, but by showing up repeatedly, mutual understanding deepens and conversations can develop into something bigger.

Some people might feel like it's a high bar to throw yourself into a new environment, but rather than regretting not jumping in, jumping in and adapting is overwhelmingly the stronger move. I've lost count of how many times I've done this kind of thing overseas this past year. Haha.

It's precisely because of that experience that even after just a few days in a new place, I can actively get involved.

People in London said to me, "It's impressive that you came to this event on only your fourth day here with no connections," but for me that's just par for the course. I'm starting to realize that what's "normal" for me isn't normal at all for others.

Still, I think this attitude of proactively diving into new environments will become even more important going forward, so I plan to keep charging ahead full speed!

excerpt_en: 2025/06/04 Well, I'm heading back to Ireland for the first time in a month. Starting May 4th, I traveled through Latvia, Lithuania, France, and the UK. It was a short period, but in each country, I managed to create opportunities for expanding the FOMUS brand.
body_en:
2025/06/04

Well, I'm heading back to Ireland for the first time in a month.

Starting May 4th,

Latvia ➡︎ Lithuania ➡︎ France ➡︎ UK

—that's the route I took.

It was a short period,

but in each country,

I managed to create opportunities that could lead to expanding the FOMUS brand.

In Latvia and Lithuania,

the Masu Photo book.

In France, the Cramaître.

In the UK, Japan House.

Of course, these are just seeds,

and the most important thing from here

is properly building and nurturing those relationships.

This is just the starting line.

Everything begins with meeting people,

but just meeting them isn't enough to make things grow.

You need to clearly communicate

who you are in a short amount of time.

In my case, being a photographer and having the masu

make a strong impact,

so I know from experience

that I can land a powerful first impression.

From here on, while traveling around the world,

I'll continue strengthening the relationships I've built so far

and also strengthen FOMUS's content.

And

I'll keep showing up at networking events.

Today is Wednesday,

so there's a Japanese meetup in Dublin tonight.

Of course, I plan to attend.

What I can do right now

is get as many people as possible to know about me

and connect that to FOMUS.

My one-year Working Holiday visa for Ireland is set to expire in about two months.

In Ireland so far,

the only concrete results I've achieved are

hosting walking events

and providing masu at Japanese Embassy events.

The collaborations I'd been thinking about since last year

with Guinness and Irish whiskey

haven't materialized yet.

Of course, this is uncharted territory

even for regular Japanese companies,

so breaking into

Ireland's business community

is incredibly difficult.

But somehow, I want to get inside that circle

and do business.

To that end,

unless some major opportunity comes calling,

I'll stay in Ireland

until my Working Holiday visa expires at the end of July.

And even after the visa expires,

I want to build an environment where I can maintain long-term relationships.

Originally, I planned to spend the entire year in Ireland,

but precisely because I realized how disconnected Japanese business owners are from the Irish business community,

I spent 2025 traveling around Europe and making my own moves.

As a result, I was even invited to an event in Paris,

so I have no regrets.

But for the remaining two months, in Ireland,

I'll use all the knowledge, connections, and skills I have

to push for results in Ireland!

Let's do this!!!

After the Meetup

The meetup started at 7 PM.

I ended up staying until the very end at 11 PM.

I've been in Ireland for about 10 months now,

so I've met tons of people along the way.

Of course, some have gone back to Japan,

but there are also people I've been close with since last year,

and hanging out with them is always fun.

Plus, I'm in a really good position—

I can talk to people in their 20s on Working Holiday

on the same level.

Of course, I don't talk about business with them,

but since they're all living overseas,

everyone has different backgrounds

and a variety of experiences,

and there's something I can respect about each of them.

And I can also communicate well

with people older than me.

That's partly because I'm a working professional

and also because I run a business.

So right now,

I'm in a position where I can easily talk to any generation.

And that makes things really comfortable.

I also introduced the Japanese university student who joined my walking event to this Japanese meetup,

and apparently they kept attending even while I was traveling to other countries.

When we met again after a month,

they told me, "I'm really glad MaSU told me about this."

When I was in my early 20s,

I also received lots of advice from people a bit older than me,

so my philosophy is to do what I can for others now.

Anyway, I'm back in Ireland.

I'm sure things will continue to be quite challenging from here,

but I'll keep valuing connections

and expanding the FOMUS brand!

excerpt_en: June 6: So today was a refresh day, and I headed to a farm and a manor house that you can reach by train from Dublin city. I went with someone I met in Ireland. I left the destination up to them, and this time it was all about seeing animals and
body_en:
June 6

So today was a refresh day,

and I headed to a farm and a manor house that you can reach by train from Dublin city.

I went with someone I met in Ireland.

I left the destination up to them,

and this time it was all about seeing animals and that kind of thing.

We met at the train platform gate,

but when we got on the train,

it unexpectedly branched off and took us somewhere completely different from our destination.

Turns out we got on the wrong train. Haha.

So,

we had to backtrack a bit.

And then,

even though we arrived at the right station,

we somehow forgot to get off

and ended up one station too far. Haha.

A trip that should've taken about 40 minutes

somehow took over an hour and a half.

I'm always riding trains and buses by myself,

and on top of that I'm always in a different environment,

so I'm usually paying close attention.

But when you're with someone (a friend),

I guess the sense of security makes you let your guard down.

Can't believe I messed up twice! Haha.

That said, we made it safely!

First up, we went to see the animals at the farm.

It's not a zoo,

so the animals are kept there,

but they're really close up, & since it was a weekday there were hardly any other visitors, so it was really easy to see everything and we had a great time.

After that, we had lunch at the on-site cafe

and joined a tour of the manor house.

I've seen plenty of fancy rooms in important houses before,

but this place also let you see the servants' quarters and such,

which gave it a different kind of appeal.

Honestly, it was a total hit,

and I think it's a hidden gem that even people living in Ireland

don't really know about.

And these kinds of opportunities to see animals and such—

unless a friend invites me,

I don't really go to places

that aren't related to business on my own,

so I was reminded once again that

this kind of time is needed every now and then.

So yeah,

it was a day of total refreshment.

In the evening I worked as usual

and went back to my normal routine.

For me right now,

this level of refresh time

seems to be just right. Haha.

title_en: Walking Event in Dublin: 11 Months Running!
excerpt_en: June 7, 2025: This month, I held another edition of my regular "Walking Event in Dublin"! Once again, about 20 people joined, and beyond the networking among participants, it's a valuable opportunity for me as the organizer to practice English, build new connections, and raise awareness of FOMUS.
body_en:
June 7, 2025:

This month, I held another edition of my regular "Walking Event in Dublin"!

Once again, about 20 people joined, and beyond the networking among participants, it's a valuable opportunity for me as the organizer to practice English, build new connections, and raise awareness of FOMUS.

People who met at this event have actually gone on trips together. I know it might sound self-serving coming from me, but I genuinely feel it's a great environment for anyone looking to make friends in Ireland.

About 80% of social events in Ireland take place at pubs in the evening, which makes it particularly hard for young women to participate.

So the format of "morning + walking + cafe," combined with the fact that the organizer is Japanese with a public personal Instagram, plus the word-of-mouth and high ratings that have built up over these 10 months, apparently provides an overwhelming sense of security.

I started this walking event by thinking about my skills and what people living in Dublin actually need, all to get FOMUS in front of as many people as possible—and it's become an event that many people are happy with!

Activities in Ireland: Moving to the Next Stage

My activities in Ireland are approaching the one-year mark.

Over this year, I've been on the ground more than anyone, accumulating countless encounters and experiences.

I'm now preparing to move to the next stage.

Honestly, I could keep running the walking event indefinitely if I wanted to.

But I'm not planning to "continue it in the same format every month" after August. That would just be maintaining the status quo, and I feel it's not enough if I want to take on bigger challenges.

By actually seeing France and the UK firsthand, I was able to feel the difference in market size on a visceral level.

This is an experience I could only gain because I used Ireland as a base and traveled the world—and it's become a tremendous asset.

excerpt_en: June 8: So, the walking event was supposed to be a two-day affair, but despite having over 30 reservations, only 2 people showed up—both of whom had also come yesterday! An unexpected turn of events! I knew same-day cancellations happen, but
body_en:
June 8

So,

the walking event was supposed to be a two-day affair,

but despite having over 30 reservations,

only 2 people showed up—both of whom had also come yesterday! An unexpected turn of events!

I knew same-day cancellations are a thing,

but this many no-shows was totally unexpected.

To prevent exactly this, I even used the survey feature on the Meetup app,

asking people to "only press the button if you're actually coming,"

but unlike yesterday, it was a total bust.

Including me, there were only 3 people who knew each other,

so I decided it wouldn't really work as an international exchange event,

and as unfortunate as it was for the 2 who showed up,

I called off the event.

Of course, those 2 could also see the reservation numbers on the Meetup app,

and they understood there was nothing I as the organizer could do about it,

so they were totally understanding.

So,

I headed back to the hotel planning to get some work done,

but I'd had this cough since around the London trip,

and it just wouldn't go away...

I was taking medicine,

but it wasn't really helping, and I was feeling drowsy,

so I decided to call it an early night.

At times like these, I know from experience that sleep

is the fastest cure.

I probably fell asleep around 3 PM. Haha.

Then I woke up around 9 PM,

ate some bread I'd bought at the supermarket,

took my medicine, and went back to sleep.

But the virus had completely

taken hold of my body,

and to fight it off, I developed a fever.

I didn't take my temperature,

but it was definitely a fever.

My body felt sluggish, my legs ached,

and all I could do was keep drinking water and flushing it out.

I had multiple things scheduled for the next day,

so there was absolutely no way

I could carry this fever into tomorrow.

I absolutely had to shake it off today.

So,

the only option was to sleep.

We'll see how it goes...

excerpt_en: June 9, 2025: The day after coming down with a fever, I had a meeting at 9 AM, so I slept until the very last minute at 8:50 and barely managed to join. The fever had completely broken, but I still had a cough. Even so, I was much better than yesterday.
body_en:
June 9, 2025:

The day after coming down with a fever, I had a meeting at 9 AM, so I slept until the very last minute at 8:50 and barely managed to join. The fever had completely broken, but I still had a cough.

Even so, I was much better than yesterday.

I somehow made it through the roughly one-hour meeting, but honestly, my mind wasn't firing on all cylinders.

Today was also checkout day at the hotel, so I packed up my things and moved to the next place.

Starting today, I was going to spend two nights and three days at the home of a Japanese friend I'd become close with in Ireland—a guy the same age as me. I took a bus about an hour and a half from Dublin to a place called Ashford.

At this point I only had a mild cough,

so it looked like I was going to survive. Haha.

From there, the two of us guys had a great time chatting about work and personal stuff.

I've left Japan and am living a life based in Ireland while flying around Europe, but there are also Japanese people my age living and working in Ireland. The fact that I got to meet him here in Ireland—someone I never would have met back in Japan—feels like it was meant to be. I want to cherish these kinds of opportunities.

Also, the fact that someone would invite a person like me—who doesn't even have a home—into their house is something I'm truly grateful for. Nothing but gratitude.

What I can give back to him right now is sharing information from my own experiences that might provide hints for his work or business.

I can't give him the answers, but I feel like I was able to share things that sparked some insights or learning for him.

Those words come from the real-life experiences of someone traveling the world and working on the ground, and there's a genuine persuasiveness in that. I think that's the "give" I'm able to offer right now.

Years from now, when I've accomplished bigger things, I want the people who are involved with me now to look back and think, "I'm glad I connected with MaSU back then!" With that in mind, I'll keep pushing forward!

excerpt_en: 2025/06/10 Day 2 in Ashford. My friend left early in the morning for work, so today I'm on my own. I headed to a town called Wicklow, near Ashford. Today's destination was one of Wicklow's tourist spots, "Wicklow Gaol."
body_en:
2025/06/10

Day 2 in Ashford. My friend left early in the morning for work, so today I'm on my own.

I headed to a town called Wicklow, near Ashford.

Today's destination was one of Wicklow's tourist spots, "Wicklow Gaol."

This prison was built in the early 18th century and for roughly 200 years housed political prisoners, the poor, and those who stole food during the famine.

During the Great Famine (1845–1852), many people were transported to Australia as convicts. The prison was then officially closed in 1924, 100 years ago, after Irish independence.

So I toured this historically significant site. By the way, everyone else had audio guides, so I tried to borrow one too, but was told, "We don't have one in Japanese." Apparently Japanese audio guides aren't very common in Ireland. Haha.

Wicklow's Great Outdoors and Walking

After touring the prison, I headed to a beautiful natural area nearby. When you leave the city center in Ireland, you find these vast expanses of nature, and I quite like it.

I could have taken a bus this time, but I chose to walk round-trip for my health—and ended up covering 15 km total! Along the way I listened to Voicy and worked on music, so it was a win all around.

Evening with My Friend

In the evening, my friend who was hosting me and I had a great time talking about work and all sorts of things. We come from very different backgrounds, but the fact that I met him in Ireland—someone I never would have crossed paths with in Japan—is truly a wonderful twist of fate. We toasted with Guinness, of course!

And we also played some video games for the first time in a while. I'd been deliberately avoiding the Nintendo Switch because I knew I'd get hooked if I picked it up.

Back in Japan, I basically only work, so I don't even have friends to play games with. But since coming to Ireland, I've played a few times—and it turns out I'm apparently terrible at action games. Haha.

The Final Sprint in Ireland

My one-year visa for Ireland has only about a month and a half left. For the remaining time, I'm going to push hard to produce results in Ireland!

excerpt_en: 2025/06/11 It's been exactly one week since I came back to Ireland. Starting the day again from my friend's place in Ashford. Got ready and had lunch together. He once pursued a career in cooking, so he's an amazing cook, and
body_en:
2025/06/11

It's been exactly one week since I came back to Ireland.

Starting the day again from my friend's place in Ashford.

Got ready

and had lunch together.

He once pursued a career in cooking,

so he's an amazing cook,

and I was treated to fantastic meals both days.

Nothing but gratitude!

I had seafood rice bowl for the first time in a while,

and it was many times more delicious than the one I had in Lithuania.

It was only two days,

but being able to spend such a meaningful time

was all thanks to my friend,

and once again, I feel so blessed to have great people in my life.

In the afternoon, I took about an hour and a half bus ride

back to Dublin and checked into a hotel.

Then, I went to pick up my suitcase

from a friend who'd been keeping it for about a month.

If I brought all my stuff

to Latvia and Lithuania,

I'd need a suitcase

and would have to pay extra luggage fees.

But since I've been living the traveler lifestyle for 4 years,

as long as I can fit what I need into a backpack and tote bag,

I can keep moving around without a suitcase.

Since I'm changing my base starting tomorrow,

I picked up all my belongings.

Then, after doing a bit of work,

I headed to the Wednesday Japanese meetup.

I deepened connections with the usual regulars

while also making the rounds to meet new people.

That said, unlike the networking events in London,

there are a lot of Japanese university students in Ireland,

and finding someone who might connect to business

is pretty tough.

With limited time,

there's no way to talk to everyone,

and I need to use my intuition

to identify the people I want to talk to,

so I'm always being selective. Haha.

Today, unfortunately, I didn't find

any major chances or connections.

But by continuing this kind of grassroots effort,

you occasionally stumble upon a big encounter.

The odds are maybe 5%,

but you just have to keep increasing your at-bats.

Man, it really is a grind every single time.

But you just gotta keep at it.

And so,

with this entry,

Part 3 of MaSU's Overseas Activity Journal comes to a close!

Starting tomorrow (2025/06/12), it's on to Part 4.

And I'll be moving my base

to Tullamore, Ireland.

I'll be doing live-in volunteer work at a historically important castle in Tullamore, while continuing to push the masu brand FOMUS out to the world.

More details in Part 4!

It's a brand-new challenge,

so I'm sure there'll be tons of lessons learned,

and I'll make sure to share them all!

Please check out Part 4 as well!!

Thank you for reading Part 3 all the way to the end!!

title_en: I'm Going to Live in a Castle
excerpt_en: 2025/06/12 Starting today, Part 4 of MaSU's European Activity Journal begins! My overseas activities started in earnest on June 21, 2024, and it's almost been a year. I've been doing all sorts of things.
body_en:
2025/06/12

Starting today,

Part 4 of MaSU's European Activity Journal begins!

My overseas activities started in earnest

on June 21, 2024,

and it's almost been a year.

I've been doing all sorts of things.

Since some people might be starting from Part 4,

let me give a quick self-introduction.

I'm MaSU.

I run a brand called "FOMUS" centered around the traditional Japanese craft of "masu" wooden cups.

I'm also active as a photographer,

an AI advisor,

and an event organizer.

Since the end of July 2024,

I've been based in Ireland, traveling around Europe,

working to spread FOMUS.

Visa-wise,

I'm here on a Working Holiday visa,

but since I have a company in Japan

and also do business individually,

I'm not employed in Ireland.

So compared to most people,

I have more freedom when it comes to travel.

Starting January 2025,

I traveled beyond Ireland to create as many connections and encounters as possible.

Once a month, I'd return to the capital, Dublin,

to run the walking events I organize,

meet up with people I'm grateful to,

and keep up those relationships.

Basically, I was all about taking action and staying on the move.

But now,

through a stroke of fate,

I've been given the opportunity to live at and volunteer at

Charleville Castle in Tullamore, Ireland.

🔻 What is Charleville Castle? Its History and Value

Charleville Castle is one of Ireland's premier examples of Gothic architecture, built in the early 19th century.

Its magnificent presence looks like something straight out of a storybook. The deep forest surrounding the castle, the moss-covered stone walls, and the interior spaces that have marked centuries of time never fail to captivate visitors.

What makes this castle particularly notable is that it contains the only surviving interior design in Ireland by William Morris, the driving force behind the Arts and Crafts movement in the late 19th century, preserved in its original state.

His philosophy of "the beauty of handcraft" permeates the space, and the moment you step inside, you feel as if time has stopped.

Furthermore, Charleville Castle's cultural and historical value has been recognized beyond national borders. Under the European cultural heritage protection treaty (the Granada Convention) and Irish law, it has been designated "Grade A."

This means it is "one of very few buildings officially protected at the highest level," a designation given to less than 2% of all structures in Ireland—making it an exceptionally rare and precious site.

Naturally, being involved with a top-ranked cultural property like this is a special opportunity granted to very few people.

🔻 A Miraculous Encounter and the Reward of Being Quick on My Feet

These 10 months since I came to Ireland have been days of relentlessly investing time and money into "meeting people." As a nomad worker without a fixed home, I've made the most of my agility, and countless encounters have colored my journey.

Then, whether by coincidence or destiny, I connected with someone affiliated with Charleville Castle. My track record of activities, quick-footed nature, and enthusiasm for international experiences were recognized, and I was offered the dream opportunity of volunteering at this historic Irish landmark!

Of course, I still have my ongoing FOMUS activities and personal work, so I'll be participating while balancing everything. However, having a fixed base this time will cut down on travel time, and I'm confident I can use my time more effectively.

🔻 Could This Be the Door to Ireland's Business Community?

This volunteer program isn't just about cultural heritage preservation.

It's actually an "Irish government-certified" program. I have a quiet hope that this could be the breakthrough into the "Irish business community" that I've been searching for these past 10 months.

Much like Broadway in America, Irish business has a very tight-knit, community-driven aspect. Unfortunately, up to this point, I haven't been able to find any Japanese business owners living in Ireland who are actively doing business with Irish entrepreneurs.

That's why finding an entry point into this "business community" has been a real struggle.

However, by participating in this government-certified volunteer program and building trust and a track record at a historic place like Charleville Castle, perhaps new business opportunities and connections with people I couldn't reach before will emerge.

I believe the only way forward is to deliver the best possible performance as a volunteer and produce results.

After August, I'll be entering a major turning point both professionally and personally, so I only have about a month and a half to fully commit to living at Charleville Castle. But with the aim of learning as much as possible from this new challenge of "protecting world cultural heritage," I intend to give everything I've got during this limited time.

🔻 First Japanese Person to Take on This Challenge, and the Real Starting Line

I will be the first Japanese person to live in and volunteer at Charleville Castle, a nationally important cultural property of Ireland! I'm incredibly excited to see what kind of days await me in this uncharted territory.

That said, very little maintenance has been done,

and the inside of the castle is unimaginably dirty. Haha.

My very first mission might just be cleaning up the room that will become my living space!

This is the real starting line of the dream-like story of living in a historic castle.

It's been 10 months since I came to Ireland. I've been running at full throttle while also traveling to other European countries, and I seized this chance. To expand the FOMUS brand and to further my own growth, I'll outwork everyone and make sure this leads to real results!
